3(a) and (b). Answer the questions in the order set. (a) Notes What do you learn about why tigers are an endangered species and what is being done to try to protect them, according to Passage B ? Write your answer using short notes. Write one point per line . You do not need to use your own words. Up to 10 marks are available for the content of your answer. Give 1 mark per point listed below, up to a maximum of 10. [10] Why tigers are an endangered species 1. Only 3,200 left in the world / population fallen by 95% in last 100 years. 2. They are hunted / killed (by poachers for their skins / body parts). 3. â€¦ because there is a demand in some cultures for luxury / medical items from tigers. 4. The tigers â€™ food supply is reduced / poachers also kill tigers â€™ natural prey. 5. The tigersâ€™ habitat is reduced / destroyed by the need for timber, agriculture, road building etc. reduce / tigers are forced into smaller living area. 6. Climate change /rising sea levels. Page 9 Mark Scheme Syllabus Paper Cambridge IGCSE â€“ May/June 2015 0500 11 Â© Cambridge International Examinations 2015 What is being done 7. WWF intend to increase pressure â€¦ 8. to classify tigers as one of the (ten) most endangered species 9. to increase patrols. 10. (work with governments) to eradicate poaching/illegal trade in tiger skins etc. 11. (work with governments) to enforce better forest management / protect the environment. 12. (work with governments) to compensate farmers whose livestock are killed by tigers. 13. (work with governments) to discourage farmers from hunting tigers that kill livestock. 3(b) Table A, Writing (concision, focus, use of own words) Use the following table to give a mark out of 5 for Writing. Band 1 5 The response is well focused on the passage and the question. All points are expressed clearly, concisely and fluently, and in the candidateâ€™s own words (where appropriate) throughout. Band 2 4 Most points are made clearly and concisely. Own words (where appropriate) are used consistently. The summary is mostly focused but may have a redundant introduction or conclusion. Band 3 3 There are some areas of conciseness. There may be occasional loss of focus or clarity. Own words (where appropriate) are used for most of the summary. Responses may be list-like or not well sequenced. Band 4 2 The summary is sometimes focused, but it may include comment, repetition, unnecessarily long explanation or lifted phrases. Band 5 1 The summary is unfocused or wordy. It may be answered in the wrong form (e.g. narrative, commentary or as notes). There may be frequent lifting of phrases and sentences. Band 6 0 Excessive lifting: no focus. The response cannot be understood or consists entirely of the words of the passage. [Total: 15] Notes on the task: In

3(a), whole sentences lifted from the passage which contain a number of points will not be credited, as they are not showing selection of points, and are not conveying the essence of the point. In

3(a), additional points added on to the bottom of the list (in addition to the 10) should not be credited unless earlier points have been removed/crossed out. In

3(a), if a point has been crossed out and not replaced with another (and it can still be read and is correct) it should be credited. In the Writing Mark Scheme descriptors for

3(b), the length of the candidateâ€™s response is no longer specifically referred to, however, it can still be borne in mind when considering the use of concision, or lack of, in the candidateâ€™s response. question_text: Imagine that you are the narrator from Passage A . After this incident you are interviewed by a local radio station about your experiences. Write a script of the interview. In the interview you should talk about: â€¢ how you and the rest of the crew came to be in the water â€¢ your thoughts and feelings when this happened â€¢ how you managed to survive the experience. Base your answers on what you have read in Passage A , but do not copy from it. Be careful to use your own words. Address each of the three bullet points. Begin the interview: Interviewer: â€˜Perhaps you could start by telling us how the boat got into trouble.â€™ Narrator: â€˜Well, we were heading towardsâ€¦â€™ Write about 200 to 300 words. mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) READING (Using and understanding the material) Use the following table to give a mark out of 10. Band 1 9â€“10 Uses and develops several ideas, both factual and inferential, from the passage. Consistently reflects the feelings of the writer and understands the reactions of the crew, the ferocity of the storm and the danger that they face from the power of the sea. Band 2 7â€“8 Refers to several details from the passage and makes some reference to the writerâ€™s feelings. Shows some awareness of the danger of the sea and not being able to progress towards the shore. Band 3 5â€“6 Repeats some details from the passage about the events in the boat. Shows some incomplete understanding of the writerâ€™s predicament and the force of the sea. Focuses on the question and on the passage, but uses material simply and partially. Band 4 3â€“4 There is some relevance to the question with a tendency to retell the original rather than to develop ideas about the storm and the sea. Makes simple references to the writerâ€™s feelings. Band 5 1â€“2 There is an attempt to use the passage. May retell the passage or give occasional relevant facts. There may be examples of misunderstanding or lack of clarity. Band 6 0 There is little or no relevance to the question or to the passage or the response copies unselectively or directly from the passage. Band 1 5 Sentences are fluent and there is a fairly wide range of vocabulary. Overall structure is good and sentences generally follow in sequence. An appropriate register is established. Band 2 4 Sentences are correct, though relatively simple. Vocabulary is adequate and correctly used. Structure is generally sound. Some of the register is appropriate. Band 3 3 Sentence structures and vocabulary are simple, but meaning is never in doubt. The order is reasonable. There may be an attempt at an appropriate register but it is inconsistent. Band 4 2 The response is very simply written and there are occasional examples of blurred meaning. The structure can usually be followed. The response may be over-dependent on lifted material. Band 5 1 The response is difficult to understand. The response may be almost entirely lifted from the original. Band 6 0 The response cannot be understood. N.B. Donâ€™t accept â€˜the tide is very strongâ€™ without reference to the effect on the boat. [1] (c) Using your own words, give three effects the tide has on the boat in which the narrator is travelling (paragraph three, â€˜Keep the boat awayâ€¦â€™). â€¢ Knocks the wind out of its sails â€¢ Spins it round â€¢ Causes it to hit the reef (so strongly that the crew fell over on deck) Allow 2 or 3 points made in one line. question_text: Imagine that you are a journalist for a national newspaper. Having interviewed the survivors from the dhow in Passage A, write a newspaper article reporting what they have told you about their experiences. In your newspaper article you should: â€¢ explain what the storm did to the dhow â€¢ describe what happened to Leo â€¢ explain how the crew escaped from this situation. Base your newspaper article on what you have read in Passage A, but do not copy from it. Be careful to use your own words. Address each of the three bullet points. Begin your newspaper article: â€˜A sudden storm caused chaos in the Indian Ocean yesterdayâ€¦â€™. Write about 200 to 300 words. We should award marks on the quality of linguistic analysis shown by the candidate in order to support her/his interpretation of the writer's purpose. â€¢ It is not necessary for candidates to show knowledge of the names of different figures of speech (simile, metaphor etc.) to produce a successful answer â€“ what we are looking for is an understanding of how the writer uses any such literary devices. â€¢ Remember that 2 is the maximum mark for any one explanation and that candidates are likely to commen

1(a) The crew of the Fairwind are described as being 'angry and disturbed'. Explain why they felt angry and why they felt disturbed (paragraph 1, â€˜On the night of...December 20th.â€™). They were angry and disturbed because: (i) the light was not working / lighthouse not in operation / dead light (1) (ii) nothing had been done about it (1) (iii) their ship had been at risk during a storm (1) 2 Note: Do not penalise selective lift of appropriate vocabulary taken from the passage but an extended verbatim lift of â€˜The crew Â« have been done about it.â€™ = 1 Award 1 mark per point to a total of 2.

1(b) Why is it thought that the authorities did not act straightaway (paragraph 1, â€˜On the night of...December 20th.â€™)? A relief ship / The Hesperus / another ship was due to sail to the lighthouse in the next few days / soon etc. 1

1(c) Why was Joseph Moore on the Hesperus and why was he restless (paragraph 2, â€˜Bad weather delayed...any breakfast.â€™)? Â• He was a relief lighthouse keeper, ready to start his spell of duty (1) Â• He was disturbed (by the reports) that the light wasn't working (1) 2

1(d) State two unusual things that the crew of the Hesperus noticed when they landed on Flannan Isle and before they entered the lighthouse (paragraph 3, â€˜Things were mysterious...the island.â€™). Â• No welcoming committee from the lighthouse keepers (1) Â• There were no boxes for fresh provisions to be seen (1) Â• No flag flying from the flagpole (1) 2 Award 1 mark per point to a total of 2. 0500/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2017 Page 4 of 14 Question Answer Marks Notes

1(e) Explain why the crew of the Hesperus were so concerned about the missing clothing and the one set of oilskins that they found in the lighthouse (lines 22â€“ 24). They were concerned because: (i) one of the crew had put himself in serious danger / done something unheard of by not wearing his protective gear (1) (ii) one / all of them had broken the rules (1) (iii) the missing outdoor gear indicated that at least two of the keepers had left the lighthouse and not come back. (1) 2 Award 1 mark per point to a total of 2.

1(f) Using your own words, explain what the writer means by: â€˜So much myth and folklore has grown up over the mystery of Flannan Isleâ€™ (line 27). Â• Myth / folklore: legend(s) / story(ies) without any basis in fact / traditional stories of a particular group of people Â• The writer is suggesting that because of the myths attached to the story it is now difficult to get to what really happened (because of embellishment / many made-up stories etc.) 2 1 mark for evidence that the vocabulary has been understood. 1 mark for an explanation similar to bullet 2. 0500/11 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2017 Page 5 of 14 Question Answer Marks Not

mark_scheme_text:
3(a) Give 1 mark per point listed below, up to a maximum of 10. Appearance and behaviour of Unicorns 1. Body of a deer/white body 2. Red hair/ blue eyes 3. Tail of an ox 4. Horse's hooves 5. Single horn (45cm / growing from the centre of its forehead) 6. Could execute a guilty person (by spearing) with its horn 7. Very swift moving/untameable 8. love for beautiful women caused it to lose fear of humans. 9. fall asleep in her lap, and could then be captured The Qualities They Possessed 10. Magical qualities/horn was a good luck charm (against scorpions). 11. Could recognise guilt 12. Ground unicorn horn a cure for many ailments 13. Shoes made from its leather assured healthy feet and legs 14. Wearing unicorn leather belts prevented plague/fever 15. Jewellery made from its horn protected wearer from evil 10 Although lifting of words and phrases from the passage is acceptable it is important that in such cases candidates show evidence of understanding by clearly focusing on the key details Over-lengthy lifting, which does not identify specific points, should not be rewarded. Credit responses which attempt to use own words and convey the essence of the point. 0500/13 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2017 Page 11 of 11 Question Answer Marks Notes Question 3b Summary Now use your notes to write a summary of what Passage B tells you about the appearance and behaviour of unicorns and of the qualities they possessed. You must use continuous writing (not note form) and use your own words as far as possible. Your summary should include all 10 of your points in Question

3(a) and must be 100 to 150 words. Up to 5 marks are available for the quality of your writing.

3(b) Table A, Writing (concision, focus, use of own words). Use the following table to give a mark out of 5 for Writing. 1 [5 marks] The response is well focused on the passage and the question. All points are expressed clearly, concisely and fluently, and in the candidateâ€™s own words (where appropriate) throughout. 2 [4 marks] Most points are made clearly and concisely . Own words (where appropriate) are used consistently. The summary is mostly focused but may have a redundant introduction or conclusion. 3 [3 marks] There are some areas of conciseness . There may be occasional loss of focus or clarity . Own words (where appropriate) are used for most of the summary. Responses may be list-like or not well sequenced. 4 [2 marks] The summary is sometimes focused , but it may include comment, repetition, unnecessarily long explanation or lifted phrases. 5 [1 marks] The summary is unfocused or wordy . It may be answered in the wrong form (e.g. narrative, commentary or as notes). There may be frequent lifting of phrases and sentences. 6 [0 marks] Excessive lifting: no focus. The response cannot be understood or consists entirely of the words of the passage. 5

Question Re-read the descriptions of: (a) the car in paragraph 5, beginning â€˜If Mamaâ€™s lime-green car â€¦â€™ (b) the atmosphere inside the car and feelings of its occupants in paragraph 20, beginning â€˜At some point in the silence that followed â€¦â€™ Select four powerful words or phrases from each paragraph. Your choices should include imagery. Explain how each word or phrase is used effectively in the context. Write about 200 to 300 words. Up to 10 marks are available for the content of your answer. 7 0500/22/M/J/17 [Turn over [Total: 10] 8 0500/22/M/J/17 Read carefully Passage B , Playing Houdini , in the Reading Booklet Insert and then answer Question 3(a) and (b) on this Question Paper. Question
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) the car The general effect is romantised/comic, possibly nostalgic, memory of decrepit vehicle. Childish imagery e.g. of cartoons reflects the now adult narrator as he was at the time. lime-green: vivid, cartoon car colour, unsophisticated colour choice crumpled like an accordion: (image) fold up, concertina, suggestive of cartoon crashes, sound effect implied, offers no resistance flimsiness: fragile, easily damaged, offering little protection suggests vulnerability of occupants listed to port or starboard: (image) rocked from side to side, as if on a boat, suggests unstable / sea sickness like sitting in a bowl of custard: wobbly, humorous image, reminiscent of clown-like behaviour squealing: shrill noise suggesting outside comfort zone / scared, old worn engine, struggling, reminiscent of terrified animal, pain looked as if it belonged in the control panel of a flying saucer: looks out of place/alien as if not fit for real purpose, suggestion of their adventure into the unknown bit savagely: painful, exaggerated to suggest action of a wild beast, emphasises lack of comfort , basic upholstery/padding, possibly danger in the experience of being in this car like lying on a bed of nails: painful and dangerous, uncomfortable, circus act, connection to magicians noble beast: like a knightâ€™s charger/ lion, romanticised term, comes to their rescue in hour of need marshmallow: squidgy and soft, no strength in car, childish 0500/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2017 Page 9 of 13 Question Answer Marks

2(b) The atmosphere inside the car and feelings of its occupants The general effect is of the tension provided by the contrast between the genuine and underplayed anxiety of mother as compared to the insensitive/immature reactions of the boys. black limousines: large imposing cars, sinister, authority, power creating a funnel : their way forward is being narrowed down, increasing pressure and stress, menace tapping the wheel: continuous small action, emphasises nervousness on the brink: (image) as if on the edge of a precipice, about to lose control temporarily deprived: deprived is emotive and refers to essentials, temporarily suggest may well get them back so emphasises that fuss is disproportionate my precious possessions: suggest they may well be of little value in reality squirtâ€™s voice providing counterpoint: (image) two voices continuous and at the same time, at different pitches, as if playing melodies in harmony with each other, unlikely though to sound pleasurable as both complaining endured: suffered as if painful/difficult litany of complaints: (image) a tedious recital / repetition of complaints over and over, builds annoyance, irritation, connection to a court of law suspicious silence: not usually silent, realising something must be wrong like a grain of sand flowing towards the neck of an hourglass: (image) slow but inevitable progress, time is running out, serious consequences ahead for them 0500/22 Cambridge IGCSE

Question Re-read the descriptions of: (a) waking up on the morning of the resignation in paragraph 1, beginning â€˜Unusually, the day I resignedâ€¦â€™ (b) the partners and their reactions to Marc in paragraph 6, beginning â€˜The partners were sittingâ€¦â€™. Select four powerful words or phrases from each paragraph. Your choices should include imagery. Explain how each word or phrase is used effectively in the context. Write about 200 to 300 words. Up to 10 marks are available for the content of your answer. 7 0500/23/M/J/17 [Turn over [Total: 10] 8 0500/23/M/J/17 Read carefully Passage B , Young Vets , in the Reading Booklet Insert and then answer Question 3(a) and (b) on this Question Paper. Question
mark_scheme_text:
2(a) (a) Waking up on the morning he was due to resign The general effect is of an unrealistic, idealised and/or humorous version of events, as if directions for a piece of theatre/film. Â• in danger of being over-rehearsed: (image) has imagined it/been through it so many times he fears he might be complacent/careless, comparison to actor giving a performance, imagined he was going to make an impact Â• cinematic fantasy: (image) unrealistic, like something from the movies, suggests he is something of a romanticist with grand ideas Â• eyes open at dawn: waking up with the sun, symbolic of a new start, new life, clichÃ©d and amusing since he usually runs late, naÃ¯ve/deluded to think this day would be different Â• slowly fades in: scene gradually appears, coming into focus, as in a film/lights on a stage Â• curtains part, revealing: bedroom curtains open, linked to idea of curtains on stage, dramatic opening, theatrical, the start of a performance Â• whizzes by: childish delight in speed of the planes , suggests his immaturity/enthusiasm Â• (trail of) rainbow-coloured smoke: vapour, colourful clouds spelling out his name, unrealistic, impossible dreams associated with rainbows and search for fortune Â• warbles: trilling associated with bird song , romanticised and ridiculous by this stage Â• What A Wonderful World: happy with life, idealised, perfect vision Â• arise: get up, emerge from sleep as if effortlessly, taking his time Â• spring in my step: lively movement ,full of energy, optimism

2(b) The partners and their reaction to Marc The general effect is that the partners are distant, ridiculously serious yet ineffective and boring, devoid of normal human emotion and/or in a fantasy world. Â• sitting in an arc around me: (image) a perfect section of a circle, mathematically precise arrangement, indicates inflexible, fussy, pedantic nature of partners, excluding/intimidating Â• like a council of elderly wizards on comfy chairs: (image) council of wizards had the potential to impress but elderly and comfy undermines that, suggestion they are past their prime and ineffective, have lost their magic Â• his eyebrow curling imperceptibly upwards: merest hint of a reaction, (hardly) bothered, not the reaction expected, scepticism Â• one grey partner to another: emphasis on dull uniformity/age of the partners, indistinguishable, lack of individuality or interest, set in their ways Â• blank expressions: emotionless , offer nothing, inhuman, anti-climax Â• hailed from different planets: (image) have nothing in common, so different to each other as if he is an alien life form to them/they are alien to anything human/fantasy world Â• never ventured far: do not travel far from home , no desire to explore, understatement to emphasise their lack of ambition/daring Â• fervently disapproved of flip-flops: over stated, intense dislike for casual footwear suggests pomposity and superficial attitude 0500/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2017 Page 8 of 11 Marking Crite

2(b) The general effect is of an enthrallingly beautiful, ethereal scene of contrasting light and magnificent, decorative architecture. huge edifice: impressively large building; cavernous structure; powerful, strong glittering and white: sparkling, new snow, pure and precious gleaming pillars (image): trees tall and slender, like columns supporting a building, suggests a fairytale castle / large cathedral / classical building; pristine delicate white tracery (image): patterns created by the snow seem intricate, deliberately and carefully crafted; fragile, temporary, pure beauty vault: arched ceiling, large space, immense height / vast size of the sky above the branches raven-black (image): complete / pitch blackness; supernatural, Gothic studded with coloured lights: stars are scattered over the sky / glints of sunlight through trees; embedded / embellished, like small pieces of bright metal or jewels, beautiful, precious 0500/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2017 Page 9 of 12 Marking Criteria for Question 2 Table A, Reading: Language analysis: Use the following table to give a mark out of 10 for Reading. Write your answers using short notes. You do not need to use your own words. Up to 10 marks are available for the content of your answer. Question Reâ€‘read the descriptions of: (a) preparing for fishing trips in paragraph 1, beginning â€˜People spend hours â€¦ â€™ (b) the canyon road in paragraph 12, beginning â€˜The road had â€¦ â€™ Select four powerful words or phrases from each paragraph. Your choices should include imagery. Explain how each word or phrase selected is used effectively in the context. Write about 200 to 300 words. Up to 10 marks are available for the content of your answer. 7 0500/22/M/J/18 [Turn over [Total: 10] 8 0500/22/M/J/18 Read carefully Passage B , The lure of fly fishing , in the Reading Booklet Insert and then answer Question 3(a) and (b) on this Question Paper. mark_scheme_text:
2(a) preparing for fishing trips The general effect is the irony of military terms used both to criticise those who prepare carefully and describe their trip. Â• (engaged in a kind of ) military manoeuvre : involved, interested, take part in exercise/ advance by soldiers; careful planning by others is considered overdone by narrator Â• gear piled strategically : equipment organised according to need or likely event , suggests planning and forethought in case of emergency but piled suggests overdone, too much if it Â• eve of our campaign : night before they set out on military exercise/set out to battle; purpose Â• comrade : friend but suggests military , alongside them in battle Â• invested heavily : spent a great deal of money as if a business venture, suggest Royer would buy equipment irrespective of cost, jumping in enthusiastically Â• flashy state-of-the-art equipment : more style over substance, suggests Royer concerned with image and not necessarily what would be useful Â• bottom-of-the-range- soft-top sports car heâ€™d borrowed: cheapest of its kind, low quality, not even his own car Â• unearthed a faded atlas: found old/out-of-date map, discovered, had been buried, not used for ages, forgotten, unclear

2(b) the canyon road The general effect is of an increasingly impassable road leading to an impressively deep canyon. Â• trickled down : reduced in size, suggests liquid movement, as if river â€“ contrast Â• a ribbon of cement-hard dirt: road is thin, narrow, unforgiving Â• rutted and rough (on the tyres ) : hard grooved, unsuitable for their vehicle, could damage tyres Â• wormed (its way): the road bends around the trees, suggests a snake or creature moving close to the ground, unattractive and unsettling image of nature conspiring against them Â• slithering between the tall trees: (image) sliding from side to side, movement around trees, reminiscent of a venomous snake, peril Â• inching forwards (cautiously) : taking particular care, moving forwards a little at a time, almost imperceptible as they are going so slowly, as if nearing the edge of something, afraid of falling Â• the trees fell away : come out of the forest suddenly, suggests the drop too Â• confronted by the magnificent pit : impressive canyon, though pit suggestive of a trap / hell Â• geological miracle : incredible natural feature, marvellous, cannot believe it has been formed out of rock, scale so amazing as if created by an act of a god Â• glittering like the tiniest trace of quicksilver : (image) seen from a distance river looks small, sparkling, mesmerising, precious; almost indistinguishable due to the depth of the canyon, like mercury, fluid, silver but only a trace, suggests fast moving and dangerous 0500/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 9 of 12 Marking Criteria for Question 2 Table A, Reading: Language analysis: Use the following table to give a mark out of 10 for Reading. Band 1 9â€“10 Wide ranging and closely focused discussion of language with some high qua

mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Harrisâ€™ behaviour and the reaction to it The general effect is of Harris as an over-bearing intruder, oblivious to the negative impact he is having on the family. nestled : settled snugly and comfortably, like a bird in a nest, cosy, safe open-beaked like an overgrown cuckoo (image) : with his mouth open ready for food like a baby bird, greedy, intruding and taking advantage of them, should not be there dangling and dancing : the baby over his head: hanging and swinging the baby about, moving her around playfully and jerkily, treating her like some kind of toy or puppet constant stream (image) : so many individual meals that cannot see where one ends and the next begins, like a constant flow of food , a small river of food there is so much of it accepted his role graciously : charming, enjoyed being a guest and being waited on like royalty extensive knowledge on an impressive range of subjects: suggests he knows a lot about a lot of different subjects, humorous as suggests this is his view only and not shared by those around him welcomed politely : understated, muted reaction suggests the real opinion is far less positive and being concealed riveted : Harris thinks the friends are interested in what he says, pinned to the spot, being polite, more shock or horror rather than genuine interest

2(b) Safeenaâ€™s attitude and Harrisâ€™ reaction to it The general effect is of Safeena fiercely defending with military precision the attempts by Harris to further encroach upon her territory and the humour in Harrisâ€™ lack of understanding of her motives. forbidden : banned, not allowed, no flexibility, preventing interference attempting the simplest chore : trying any kind of task around the house; reflect his infirmity (real or imagined) inability, lack of enthusiasm to do even the most basic job (properly) swivelled kitchen-wards : eyes turned, looked in the general direction of the kitchen, mechanical , birdlike; humorous, cartoonish materialise (from nowhere) (image) : appear suddenly, as if by magic, teleport like an alien, unspoken battle between them, monitoring him intercepting him : getting between him and his goal, like a missile or defence shield, defending her domain (crossing the lounge was) a hazardous run : dangerous moving across the lounge, a challenge in itself, as if sailing or on a mission; little intention of moving booby-trapped : items left deliberately to catch someone out, left by enemy sharp outcrops of plastic play-bricks (image): toys left on floor, pointy, pierce foot if walked on, act like defences (safer to stay) moored (image) : not moving from the sofa, boat tied in a harbour to stop floating off, secure, calm, lazy, protected showered (them) : giving them lots of money, happening at intervals, generosity is over-done 0500/23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2018 Page 10 of 13 Marking Criteria for Question 2 Table A, Reading: Language analysis: Use the following table to give a mark out of 10 for Reading. mark_scheme_text: 2 (a) The world outside Mikaelaâ€™s window The general effect is to emphasise Mikaelaâ€™s feelings of loneliness and exclusion. watched the world pass by the window (image): observes people outside, as if excluded from everyone else on the planet; cinematic panorama, spectator liberated pedestrians: free to walk, Mikaelaâ€™s desire to be like them/free of current restrictions drawn into a current (image): pulled in one direction together, swept along like a body of water, irresistible force bags bounced and bounded behind them: bumping along the uneven ground , leaping up and down, childlike exuberance, excited, animated, carefree (in) enthusiastic obedience (image): wheeled shopping bags follow like pets on a leash, Mikaelaâ€™s own wish that she wasnâ€™t restricted. a sea of movement (image): a large body of people walking purposefully in the same direction, force, tide, overwhelming numbers that belong to the world she canâ€™t join and which have left her behind. imprisoned figure (in the window) (image): no-one raised their eyes /moved their heads in her direction, do not think about her, abandoned, captive, character in a fairy-tale (b) The scene at the market The general effect is that a visit to the market is an assault to the senses â€“ exciting and potentially dangerous. the market was beginning to wake up (image): starting the day, signs of activity, gaining consciousness like a large beast (image): huge creature, impressive size of market, monstrous, dangerous, dragon, mythical groaning in dismay at being prodded into life (image): mix of low, deep sounds of the stalls and awnings being erected, hard work, effort, difficulty elongated body undulating and rippling (image): line of awnings stretching into the distance, billowing motion, wavelike fluttering (awnings and canvas covers): quick light movements, winged creature (metal trollies) squealed in angry protest (image): high pitched noise of grinding metal wheels, rebelling, apparent hostility, resistance to work careered crazily: uncoordinated, fast headlong dash, out of control, chaotic butted up against the stalls: reached a dead end, come to a final halt disgorged their contents (image): poured out goods inside , vomiting slipping and sliding across shiny surfaces: skidding, uncontrolled movement 0500/21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED mark_scheme_text:
2(a) Wusani and her experience at Bush Camp in paragraph 2 The general effect is cartoon-like, with a stereotypical figure and exaggerated comic actions. This creates humour at Wusaniâ€™s expense. decrepit, spluttering truck: (image) intermittent engine sounds of the aged truck, expressing outrage at being expected to carry heavy load and Wusani on top of that bulk: mass of something large, difficult and heavy to move made the ageing truckâ€™s suspension creak ominously: sound suggests the old vehicle about to break under Wusaniâ€™s weight, sense of foreboding particularly disliked: specifically something she did not like, understated emotion suggests actually was far worse than that and her feelings were demonstrated loudly unpleasantly surprised: had suddenly heard the lion, actually likely to have been more shocked and scared, understated to suggest the reaction was in fact extreme any less terrifying: even though she was unlikely to be in real danger Wusani was extremely scared , contrast with disliked and surprised to suggest in his view her reaction was disproportionate and extreme improbably perched: (image) she is stuck up the tree on a very thin branch, has gone to the highest / furthest branch she could, precarious, ridiculous picture comparing her unfavourably to a bird adrenaline had fuelled the climb: (image) her fear had given her the energy to climb higher / faster than she would have otherwise been able to do, burst of energy like a rocket gravityâ€™s pull resolved the issue: she fell down from the tree, humour as it implies rather than describes the event saved from serious harm: she was not badly injured, received only minor injuries as a result of the fall, never in any real danger

2(a) The zookeeper in paragraph 1 The general effect is of the physical signs of extreme aging and the biological maturity/deterioration of the zookeeper â€“ a curiosity for the boy as if observing another species. hobbling (along): limping painfully, restricted movements due to age, struggling, sign of physical deterioration, weakened aged man: very old human, has matured, grown older naturally, wisdom emit a pained grunt: involuntary sound suggests the work is difficult and painful for him, noise more typical of an animal gnarled knuckles: (image) roughened as from old age or work, knotty like an old tree trunk ropy tendons and veins (stood out) : (image) connecting fibres and blood vessels are no longer supple but thickened with age, evidence of exertion tissue-thin skin: almost transparent, delicate, gauze-like, natural cells of a biological organism, easily torn mottled (with age) : spotted, blotched, evidence of older skin, effects of time inhabited by ghosts of forgotten injuries: (image) haunting memories only fleeting now, possibly evidence of encounters with real animals, troubled by memories secret stories of wounds: (image) injuries have healed leaving scars â€“ process of healing naturally, mysteries of natural processes are not known to boy baggy covering: (image) too big to fit, outer layer, concealing what is beneath, as if can be taken off (feel it slide over the) brittle bones: fragile, easily broken, frailty time-withered muscle: dry, shrivelled with extreme age, dying 0500/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2019 Page 10 of 15 Question Answer Marks

2(b) The animal in paragraph 14 The general effect is of a museum specimen / curiosity from a bygone age â€“ describes the effects of time and technology on an animal long since dead. The identity of the animal is not yet revealed; condition invites comparison to zookeeper. ancient beastâ€™s pelt: skin or hide of an animal only, as if trophy or exhibit of part of prehistoric or mythological creature bleached bone-white by unrelenting sun: colour has been removed over time by unforgiving sun, vitality and life has been lost, unnatural badly tattered: torn to shreds, ragged and worn, over-used bare spots betrayed: truth revealed by damage , let down as not a (wholly) real lion fur rubbed painfully against metal: contrast of natural substance and unyielding material, raw suffering of beast, empathy of zookeeper exposing old tears: revelation of damage previously sustained since exhibited, has worn out a long time ago raggedly-sutured gaps: repeated attempts to patch, stitches obvious now as so threadbare that there are holes between stitches, amateurish, attempt to care underlying musculature: system of muscles beneath the skin of a body, reminiscent of medical specimen atrophied meat: wasted, withered, shrivelled â€“ contrast to lab grown meat churning gears: disks and wheels turn over mechanically, routinely, mechanism evident, suggests tired of same movement (churns out) 0500

2(a) Stanâ€™s memories of his school classroom in paragraph 4, beginning â€˜A stern face Â« â€™ The general effect is that Stan remembers the shame of his failure to succeed in English when younger. Â• stern face presiding over ranks of desks (image): remembers the serious expression of teacher, authoritarian, military leader, rows of soldiers, power, control, stifling creativity, Stanâ€™s fear/vulnerability Â• pulled (young Stan) to attention: got his complete and total focus, military command, suggesting speed with which Stan reacts Â• (completely) obliterate: annihilate, utterly destroy, Stanâ€™s terror. Â• unworthy existence: doesnâ€™t deserve to live, little merit Â• (like) an army cadetâ€™s besmirched tunic buttons (image): ashamed of poor results, unfit for military parade, exposed to the derision of others and/or anger of his elders/superiors Â• emblematic (of deep failure and shame): signalling his lack of success for all to see, symbol of disgrace Â• underline: emphasises, drawing attention to, shows up his error Â• demotion to an even lesser division (image): relegate, he will be dropped to a lower class/position; draws attention to his shame Â• contorted: twisted (in an ugly way); malice, derision, cruelty of former teacher Â• triumphant smirk: victorious, smug smile, teacher takes pleasure in Stanâ€™s lack of success Â• poured over him like hot coals (image): words caused emotional anguish, physical burning pain, causes deep scarring

2(b) What happened at break-time in paragraph 16, beginning â€˜Suddenly a teapot Â« â€™ The general effect is of an overly dramatic and/or humorous reaction to a relatively minor incident. 2(a) The sightings in Ungava Bay in paragraph 2, beginning â€˜The adventure Â« â€™ The general effect is of a mysterious, grand and potentially hostile landscape. Â• first glimpse: quick, short look (of a polar bear), tantalising Â• eerie ghost ship of an iceberg (appeared) (image): resembled a mysterious empty sea vessel, adrift and abandoned, haunting apparition Â• loomed closer: coming into nearer view; overshadowing, threatening Â• hulking yellowy-white bear : heavy, clumsy, bulky, dirty-coloured, unattractive, suggests power/huge size; contrast with white backdrop/expectations Â• lumbering heavily: slow awkward deliberate motion due to size, takes great effort to move forwards, struggling Â• blinding ice: pure white and dazzling, painful to look at, inhospitable environment Â• (iceberg slowly) twisted and turned: gyrating, moving around, mesmerising Â• revealed: allowed the island to be seen, no longer secret Â• vast flocks: huge groups, impressive, outnumber the tourists, overwhelming, imposing Â• billowing out in black, stormy clouds (image): a dark mass of birds, rising and swelling, blurred shapes, menacing Â• (eyed them with) cold-eyed disdain: unemotional , personified as unfriendly and contemptuous, ruthless, gulls consider themselves superior to the murres 0500/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 10 of 14 Question Answer Marks

2(b) Adam and his actions in paragraph 3, beginning â€˜We did most of our sailing Â« â€™ The general effect is of Adamâ€™s melodramatic performance as he pretends to be looking-out for polar bears. Â• intrepid polar bear monitor : brave adventurer, responsibility for spotting bears, playing a (self-designated) role, self-importance Â• painstakingly picked out: very carefully selected, taking time to identify exact route; exaggerating potential danger Â• as regularly as a pendulum (image): swinging in a smooth, rhythmical manner from side to side; mechanical, set pattern, suggesting a need for wariness Â• flicked: quick, minimal shake, conducting the tourist group Â• command our silence and immediate cessation (of movement): ordering them to stop talking and walking, sense of urgency, expectation of compliance, over-bearing, imperious Â• cup his ear and lean: motioning as if listening, caricature, theatrical Â• we presumed such a creature lurked: Adamâ€™s actions have created the fiction of a terrifying beast waiting to ambush the tourists Â• (at once) alert and apprehensive: giving the appearance of being watchful, anticipating danger, staged fear Â• circle back Â« stealthily pad round us as protective as a mother lion (image): slow and quiet movements around them as if to make sure they are safe, as if fiercely defending cubs; potentially predatory, cunning Â• (issuing a) broad wink : a dramatic, pronounced act of collusion; conspiracy, enjoying the joke, sharing it with his audience 0500/22 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2019 Page 11 of 14 Marking Criteria for Question 2 Table A, Read

question_text: Identify a word or phrase from the text which suggests the same idea as the words underlined: (a) Boats used for recreational purposes were moored in the harbour as well as the boats used by local fishermen. (i) [1] The colours of the boats and houses by the harbour were bright and cheerful. (ii) [1] At the time of the incident, rules about where oil tankers could wash out their tanks were not as strict as they are now. (iii) [1] Oil tanks washing out their tanks left huge patches of sea covered with a film of poisonous oil. question_text: Imagine that you are a student at another school in the same district as Champion School. Your school is considering whether to follow the same plan as Champion School or not. Write a speech to give to a meeting of parents and teachers from your school about why all-year- round schooling would not be a good idea. In your speech you should: â€¢ evaluate the ideas, attitudes and opinions about all-year-round schooling given in the texts â€¢ explain your concerns about changing to all-year-round schooling and how it would affect parents, teachers and the wider community. Base your speech on what you have read in both texts, but be careful to use your own words. Address both of the bullet points. Write about 250 to 350 words. Up to 15 marks are available for the content of your answer, and up to 25 marks for the quality of your writing. 3 0500/23/O/N/22 [Turn over 4 0500/23/O/N/22 [Total: 40] 5 0500/23/O/N/22 [Turn over [Turn to page 6 for Section B] 6 0500/23/O/N/22 Section B: Composition Answer one question from Section B. mark_scheme_text: Question 1 This question tests the following writing assessment objectives (25 marks) W1 articulate experience and express what is thought, felt and imagined W2 organise and structure ideas and opinions for deliberate effect W3 use a range of vocabulary and sentence structures appropriate to context W4 use register appropriate to context W5 make accurate use of spelling, punctuation and grammar and reading assessment objectives (15 marks) R1 demonstrate understanding of explicit meanings R2 demonstrate understanding of implicit meanings and attitudes R3 analyse, evaluate and develop facts, ideas and opinions, using appropriate support from the text R5 select and use information for specific purposes. 0500 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 5 of 12 Question Answer Marks 1 Imagine that your friend has an ambitious plan for their future and has asked for your views. Write a letter to your friend offering your advice. In your letter you should: â€¢ evaluate the ideas, attitudes and opinions about ambition given in both texts â€¢ based on what you have read, discuss the factors your friend needs to consider to decide if they should follow their ambition. Base your letter on what you have read in both texts, but be careful to use your own words. Address both of the bullet points. Write about 250 to 350 words. Up to 15 marks are available for the content of your answer, and up to 25 marks for the quality of your writing. Responses might use the following ideas: Text A â€¢ c ommon term, means different things â€¢ some ambitions are ridiculous/unlikely â€¢ ambition is sometimes socially conditioned â€¢ ambitions dictate what you do and affect your sense of self â€¢ everyone thinks ambition is necessary for success â€¢ expectation is different to ambition â€¢ ambition needs to be realistic â€¢ media tells us we can succeed â€¢ comparatively few people reach their ambitions Text B â€¢ ambition is considered to be generally positive â€¢ too much ambition can be counter productive â€¢ narrow focus is not good â€“ you can miss out â€¢ makes you selfish and affects relationships â€¢ can make you inflexible â€¢ want to do too much too quickly â€¢ failure should be seen as a learning experience â€¢ more ambition means material success but not necessarily much happiness or better health 40 0500 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2023 Page 6 of 12 Question Answer Marks 1 Possible evaluation of ideas / inference: Factors to consider might include: â€¢ e xcessive ambition may adversely affect (mental) health : is it worth it? â€¢ a mbition is important for your mental health â€¢ a mbition should include family / friends or it is not worth it â€¢ i s it your own ambition or imposed by others / society? â€¢ a re you naturally ambitious / driven? â€¢ w ill this ambition close down other/better possibilities? â€¢ d o you have the ability / experience to succeed in your ambition : unrealistic otherwise â€¢ h ow will you cope with failure ?: c an be demotivating â€¢

mark_scheme_text: Question 1 This question tests the following writing assessment objectives (25 marks) W1 articulate experience and express what is thought, felt and imagined W2 organise and structure ideas and opinions for deliberate effect W3 use a range of vocabulary and sentence structures appropriate to context W4 use register appropriate to context W5 make accurate use of spelling, punctuation and grammar and reading assessment objectives (15 marks) R1 demonstrate understanding of explicit meanings R2 demonstrate understanding of implicit meanings and attitudes R3 analyse, evaluate and develop facts, ideas and opinions, using appropriate support from the text R5 select and use information for specific purposes. 0500 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 6 of 12 Question Answer Marks 1 Write an article for your school or college magazine about whether or not a volunteer scheme for older students would be a good idea. In your article you should: â€¢ evaluate the ideas, attitudes and opinions about volunteering in both texts â€¢ consider the effects a compulsory volunteering scheme in school would have on students, teachers and the wider community. Base your article on what you have read in both texts, but be careful to use your own words. Address both of the bullet points. Write about 250 to 350 words. Up to 15 marks are available for the content of your answer and up to 25 marks for the quality of your writing. Notes on task: Responses might use the following explicit ideas: Text A â€¢ the previous scheme was unpopular â€¢ there are aspects missing in young peopleâ€™s lives â€“ compassion, service â€¢ will need vetted settings â€¢ will be unsupervised / cannot opt out â€¢ volunteering should have parity with other subjects â€¢ time will need to be found in the curriculum Text B â€¢ volunteering means giving up time/many people do this informally â€¢ resentful students do not make good volunteers â€¢ adults can choose how and where to volunteer â€¢ modern charity workers are paid and trained â€¢ learning to help at home might be more valuable Possible evaluation of ideas: â€¢ learning independence is valuable for studentsâ€™ futures. â€¢ compulsory volunteering is contradictory / misses its real value. ( Do not accept â€˜it shouldnâ€™t be compulsoryâ€™ on its own.) â€¢ teachers will have less time to teach the curriculum / could ease their workload (Both valid inferences) â€¢ admin of checking placements is difficult, time - consuming â€¢ academics should not be jeopardised / may impact studentsâ€™ achievement â€¢ students do not have the maturity to help vulnerable people/too much responsibility for young people/could do harm â€¢ charity workers will look after students, not the people who need care â€¢ a bad experience might put students off charity work for life â€¢ may lead to careers in charity organisations 40 0500 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED May/June 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 7 of 12 Markin

question_text: Imagine you live in a city which is considering redesigning your neighbourhood. Write a letter to the city authorities, giving your views on the proposal to create a â€˜city villageâ€™. In your letter you should: â€¢ evaluate the ideas, attitudes and opinions given in the texts â€¢ consider whether or not the proposal is desirable or workable and whether or not it will benefit different people in the community. Base your letter on what you have read in both texts, but be careful to use your own words. Address both of the bullet points. Write about 250 to 350 words. Question 1 This question tests the following writing assessment objectives (25 marks) W1 articulate experience and express what is thought, felt and imagined W2 organise and structure ideas and opinions for deliberate effect W3 use a range of vocabulary and sentence structures appropriate to context W4 use register appropriate to context W5 make accurate use of spelling, punctuation and grammar and reading assessment objectives (15 marks) R1 demonstrate understanding of explicit meanings R2 demonstrate understanding of implicit meanings and attitudes R3 analyse, evaluate and develop facts, ideas and opinions, using appropriate support from the text R5 select and use information for specific purposes. 0500 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 6 of 12 Question Answer Marks 1 Imagine a close friend is trying to make a change in their life but is finding it hard to do so. Write a letter to your friend giving your advice. In your letter you should: â€¢ evaluate the ideas in both texts and suggest why you think your friend might be finding it difficult to make the change â€¢ suggest to your friend some of the things they could do to help them to make the change. Base your letter on what you have read in both texts, but be careful to use your own words. Address both of the bullet points. Write about 250 to 350 words. Up to 15 marks are available for the content of your answer and up to 25 marks for the quality of your writing. Notes on task: Responses might use the following ideas: Text A â€¢ Habits are formed over a long time eval: takes time to change them â€¢ Success depends on how many obstacles are in your way eval: we (canâ€™t) expect instant rewards â€¢ Things we crave have changed over time eval: make sure what you crave is valuable (are â€˜money, fame, approvalâ€™ really the best things to crave?) â€¢ Wonâ€™t do something if you are not motivated enough or it is too hard eval: find enjoyable steps to help you stick to them â€¢ People donâ€™t like failure eval : (donâ€™t) give up too easily, takes perseverance â€¢ Habits are formed unconsciously eval: need to make conscious choices about/identify the habits that are good/bad for us/what we believe in Text B â€¢ There are people who are paid to affect our thinking eval: be aware that they might not mean what they say/be self - interested â€¢ Social media influencers make you feel like you know them : eval: people in real life are likely to be more trustworthy/useful â€¢ Influencers present a curated a view of their lives: eval can lead to unrealistic aspirations in their followers â€¢ Social media influencers can be useful: eval: donâ€™t rely on other â€¢ people/change for yourself, no one else â€¢ Humans are social and copy those around them eval: choose influencers carefully 40 0500 / 21 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 7 of 12 Marking criteria for Section A

Write a letter to your friend giving your advice. In your letter you should: â€¢ evaluate the ideas and opinions in the text about attitudes to making mistakes â€¢ suggest to your friend the best way to approach day one to help them feel less nervous and less worried. Base your letter on what you have read in the text, but be careful to use your own words. Address both of the bullet points. Write about 250 to 350 words. Notes on task: Responses might use the following ideas: Text A Attitudes to making mistakes: â€¢ Some people deny their mistakes eval : others can see through it â€¢ Some people make mistakes they tell others not to make eva l: morally wrong, arrogant to not acknowledge mistakes â€¢ Should learn from your mistake eval: necessary part of professional development â€¢ Some mistakes are bigger than others eval: unlikely in part - time job for mistakes to be serious, probably trivial â€¢ Looking confident can create real confidence eval : can badly affect your mental health/could be viewed as dishonest, self - centred/you might miss chances to improve â€¢ Not admitting mistakes can be harmful eval : will damage your reputation/ cause injustice to others/others will resent it Advice to friend about their first day: â€¢ Fake it til you make it eval: preserving your image over performance is wrong â€¢ Appear happy and confident eval: people will be tolerant on your first day â€¢ Smiling will help your mood and others will be friendlier eval : a good professional quality to appear willing, approachable 40 0500 / 23 Cambridge IGCSE â€“ Mark Scheme PUBLISHED October/November 2025 Â© Cambridge University Press & Assessment 2025 Page 7 of 12 Marking criteria for Section A
